stage.name: AXI boundary: where CVA6 becomes a system component
stage.description: A boundary lesson on AXI channels, required signals, atomic support, and the manager/subordinate split.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: AXI is the handoff from core story to system story

content.title: AXI is the handoff from core story to system story
content.subtitle: Once memory leaves the core-local explanation, AXI becomes the contract that other system blocks can understand.
content.thesis: Do not explain AXI as another pipeline stage; explain it as the external memory-interface contract.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: AXI interface
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 uses AXI as the memory interface, implements required AXI signals, supports AXI4 features and the AXI5 atomic operation feature with restrictions.

content.speakerNotes: This lesson is AXI boundary: where CVA6 becomes a system component. Once memory leaves the core-local explanation, AXI becomes the contract that other system blocks can understand. The point is not to memorize a wall of CVA6 facts. The point is to build a disciplined source-reading habit: name the boundary, name the source, and avoid claims that the public material does not support.

actions.text: This lesson is AXI boundary: where CVA6 becomes a system component. Once memory leaves the core-local explanation, AXI becomes the contract that other system blocks can understand. The point is not to memorize a wall of CVA6 facts. The point is to build a disciplined source-reading habit: name the boundary, name the source, and avoid claims that the public material does not support.

scenes.title: CVA6 is the AXI manager for memory

content.title: Manager and subordinate are system roles
content.subtitle: The AXI chapter says CVA6 initiates the memory connection.

columns.title: CVA6 side
columns.body: CVA6 sends requests through a manager interface toward memory.

columns.title: System side
columns.body: Memory or interconnect behaves as the subordinate side of the transaction.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: AXI interface
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 uses AXI as the memory interface, implements required AXI signals, supports AXI4 features and the AXI5 atomic operation feature with restrictions.

content.speakerNotes: Unit 08 starts outside the pipeline. The AXI chapter describes CVA6 as the initiator of memory transactions, so CVA6 has the manager role and the memory system is subordinate. This matters because LSU behavior and AXI fabric behavior are not the same explanation. The LSU prepares memory operations; AXI carries requests and responses across a system boundary.

actions.text: Unit 08 starts outside the pipeline. The AXI chapter describes CVA6 as the initiator of memory transactions, so CVA6 has the manager role and the memory system is subordinate. This matters because LSU behavior and AXI fabric behavior are not the same explanation. The LSU prepares memory operations; AXI carries requests and responses across a system boundary.

scenes.title: Read AXI as channels, not as one giant wire

content.title: Separate address, data, and response phases
content.subtitle: The AXI chapter emphasizes separate address/control and data phases.

cards.title: Write address
cards.body: AW signals identify the write transaction address and attributes.

cards.title: Write data
cards.body: W signals carry write data and byte strobes.

cards.title: Read path
cards.body: AR and R channels separate read request from read data response.

content.takeaway: Channel separation is why AXI can support latency and outstanding work.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: AXI interface
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 uses AXI as the memory interface, implements required AXI signals, supports AXI4 features and the AXI5 atomic operation feature with restrictions.

content.speakerNotes: A learner should not read AXI as one massive bundle. AXI separates address, data, and response phases, and that separation is exactly why the protocol can support high-frequency systems, latency, and outstanding transactions. The official CVA6 chapter then adds restrictions. So the professional reading is: first protocol shape, then CVA6-specific support, then system integration.

actions.text: A learner should not read AXI as one massive bundle. AXI separates address, data, and response phases, and that separation is exactly why the protocol can support high-frequency systems, latency, and outstanding transactions. The official CVA6 chapter then adds restrictions. So the professional reading is: first protocol shape, then CVA6-specific support, then system integration.

scenes.title: The testharness shows the integration picture

content.title: CVA6 becomes one master among many mapped components
content.body: The APU testharness connects ariane/CVA6, debug, DRAM, CLINT, PLIC, ROM, UART, timer, and other blocks through AXI-related wiring.
content.caption: Use the testharness diagram to move from core to system.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 APU: testharness
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The testharness connects CVA6, debug, CLINT, PLIC, timer, ROM, UART, DRAM, and other slaves through AXI-related structures.

content.speakerNotes: The APU testharness is useful because it makes the boundary visible. CVA6 is no longer just a pipeline diagram. It is one component connected to memory, debug, interrupt, ROM, timer, UART, and other blocks. Some details in that chapter are testharness-specific, so we should not overgeneralize it, but it is excellent for seeing what integration means.

actions.text: The APU testharness is useful because it makes the boundary visible. CVA6 is no longer just a pipeline diagram. It is one component connected to memory, debug, interrupt, ROM, timer, UART, and other blocks. Some details in that chapter are testharness-specific, so we should not overgeneralize it, but it is excellent for seeing what integration means.

scenes.title: Check understanding: AXI boundary: where CVA6 becomes a system component

questions.question: In the AXI memory-interface explanation, what role does CVA6 play?
questions.analysis: The AXI chapter describes CVA6 as initiating the connection with memory.

questions.question: Which AXI ideas should stay separate in a careful explanation?
questions.analysis: RAS prediction is frontend behavior, not an AXI channel.

questions.question: What is the biggest boundary mistake to avoid?

options.label: Treating AXI as a pipeline stage inside issue

questions.analysis: AXI is an external memory-interface contract, not an issue-stage pipeline step.

questions.question: Which source is best for seeing CVA6 in a SoC-like wiring context?
questions.analysis: The testharness connects CVA6 to memory, debug, CLINT, PLIC, timer, ROM, and other blocks.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: AXI interface
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 uses AXI as the memory interface, implements required AXI signals, supports AXI4 features and the AXI5 atomic operation feature with restrictions.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements: interface requirements
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements document names MEM, DBG, IRQ, XIF, and TRI requirements, including AXI memory interface, CLINT interrupt interface, CV-X-IF, and P-Mesh TRI.

sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The testharness connects CVA6, debug, CLINT, PLIC, timer, ROM, UART, DRAM, and other slaves through AXI-related structures.

content.speakerNotes: This quiz checks the core judgment of AXI boundary: where CVA6 becomes a system component. The right answers should feel less like trivia and more like disciplined classification: what is the boundary, what is the source, what is the limitation, and what would we check next?

scenes.title: Summary: AXI boundary: where CVA6 becomes a system component

content.title: AXI boundary: where CVA6 becomes a system component
takeaways: Explain CVA6 AXI support without confusing core-internal LSU behavior with SoC memory-fabric behavior.
Turn every public explanation into an evidence-backed learning artifact.
content.nextStep: Next: interrupt and debug boundaries, where external events become precise architectural control flow.
content.closing: This is how OpenHW Explorer can teach serious open hardware without drifting into unsupported certainty.

content.speakerNotes: To summarize: Explain CVA6 AXI support without confusing core-internal LSU behavior with SoC memory-fabric behavior. Keep the claim small, keep the source visible, and keep the boundary honest. Next: interrupt and debug boundaries, where external events become precise architectural control flow.
